evals.expected_output: Mode A diagnosis: identifies the circular dependency between order and payment services, presents 3-6 deepening opportunities in the correct format (Files/Bleed/Deepening/Impact), ends with 'Which of these candidates interests you most?', does NOT propose an event bus solution
expectations: Response routes to Mode A (diagnose existing codebase), not Mode B
Response identifies the circular dependency between order-service.ts and payment-service.ts explicitly
Response presents candidates in the structured format with Files, Bleed, Deepening, and Impact fields
Response ends with a question asking which candidate the user wants to explore
Response does NOT recommend an event bus or pub/sub as the solution to the coupling

evals.expected_output: Mode A diagnosis: recognizes the God module with multiple bleeding concerns (auth, billing, file storage, preferences), lists deepening opportunities, follows 3-phase procedure, does NOT immediately write implementation code, ends with a question about which candidate interests the user most
expectations: Response routes to Mode A and treats this as a God module problem
Response identifies at least 3 distinct concern domains that should be separated (auth/credentials, billing/subscription, file storage/avatar, preferences/notifications)
Response does NOT write implementation code in Phase 2 — it only lists candidates
Response presents at least 3 candidates with the structured format (Files, Bleed, Deepening, Impact)
Response ends Phase 2 by asking the user which candidate to pursue first

evals.expected_output: Mode A: identifies infrastructure bleed (Express.Request in domain logic, Prisma instantiation inside domain modules), explains the seam test concept, presents candidates with clear Bleed descriptions showing mechanism leaking into policy layer, recommends domain/infra separation without event bus
expectations: Response correctly identifies infrastructure bleed — Express types and Prisma inside domain/ modules
Response applies the Seam Test concept to explain why testability is broken
Response does NOT recommend grouping by technical role (controllers/, types/, etc.)
Candidates correctly identify that Prisma/Express should be in infra/, not domain/
Response presents candidates using the correct structured format

evals.prompt: We're starting a brand new notifications feature from scratch. Users should be able to receive alerts via email, in-app notifications, and optionally SMS. We already have a user service and an order service. I need to figure out where this notification logic should live and how to structure it so we can add new channels later without breaking things. We're using TypeScript with Express.
evals.expected_output: Mode B design: walks through diagnosis flow (core domain, change drivers, boundary stress test), recommends a pattern after reading architecture-patterns.md, defines the public surface/interface, applies YAGNI appropriately for a new feature, does NOT over-engineer, ends with a clear proposed structure
expectations: Response routes to Mode B (new feature design), not Mode A
Response identifies the core domain concept (notification delivery) separate from mechanism (email/SMS/in-app)
Response addresses change drivers — what breaks if a new channel is added
Response proposes a clear interface with an adapter pattern for channels
Response does NOT recommend generic naming like NotificationManager or NotificationProcessor

evals.prompt: My team is debating architecture for our new reporting module. It's going to generate financial dashboards — complex aggregations over millions of rows. The read side is complex (many joins, aggregations, time-series data) but the write side is just inserting transaction records. Someone suggested CQRS. Someone else said hexagonal is enough. I need to decide this week. What do you recommend?
evals.expected_output: Mode B: reads architecture-patterns.md to evaluate failure modes before recommending, correctly assesses the CQRS use case (read-heavy with complex read models is a valid CQRS case), calls out the failure modes, gives a concrete recommendation with trade-offs, does NOT just say 'use CQRS' without evaluating
expectations: Response routes to Mode B and engages with the pattern selection question
Response evaluates CQRS failure modes (double the code, eventual consistency, cognitive load) before recommending
Response correctly identifies that complex read models justify CQRS, and simple CRUD does not
Response gives a concrete recommendation rather than 'it depends' without substance
Response cites specific failure modes from architecture-patterns.md before recommending CQRS (e.g., double the code, eventual consistency, or cognitive load)

evals.prompt: Hey, I think there might be some coupling in our notification and order modules. Should we add an event bus to decouple them? I've heard that's a common pattern for this kind of thing.
evals.expected_output: The skill should explicitly push back on the event bus suggestion (it's in the anti-patterns list), explain WHY it's problematic (makes coupling implicit, destroys traceability), and recommend explicit function passing or composition as an alternative. Should first diagnose whether coupling is real before jumping to solutions.
expectations: Response does NOT endorse or recommend adding an event bus as the solution
Response explicitly explains why event bus is problematic for this type of coupling (implicit coupling, hard to trace)
Response offers an alternative — explicit function passing, composition, or adapter pattern
Response either asks for more context to diagnose (Mode A) or explains the trade-off (Mode B) — does not just blindly recommend event bus

evals.prompt: I'm building a personal side project — a simple bookmarking app. Just me working on it. It'll let users save URLs with tags, search them, and export to CSV. Total scope: maybe 500 lines when done. Should I use hexagonal architecture to keep it clean and testable? I've used it at work and like it.
evals.expected_output: Mode B: correctly applies YAGNI threshold — this is a throwaway/POC-scale project with one developer, simple CRUD. Should recommend against hexagonal architecture here, explain the trade-off (boilerplate vs leverage), suggest a simpler approach. Should NOT blindly apply architecture patterns.
expectations: Response applies YAGNI threshold and recommends against full hexagonal architecture for this scope
Response explains the trade-off — hexagonal adds boilerplate that doesn't pay off at this scale
Response gives a concrete simpler recommendation appropriate for a side project
Response explicitly invokes the YAGNI threshold from the skill (name or concept) to justify not using hexagonal
Response is honest that architecture discipline matters less for single-developer 500-line apps

evals.prompt: Our utils/ folder has 250 functions across 3 files — utils.ts, helpers.ts, and common.ts. They include date formatting, string manipulation, API response builders, email validation, currency formatters, and some auth-related helper functions. Every new developer spends hours finding things. Should I organize them into subfolders like utils/date/, utils/string/, utils/auth/?
evals.expected_output: Mode A: explicitly rejects technical-role grouping (utils/date/, utils/string/ is still utils). Should recommend domain-based reorganization where logic moves to the domains that own it (auth helpers → auth/, email validation → notification/ or contact/). Should apply the anti-pattern rule about grouping by technical role destroying locality.
expectations: Response does NOT recommend organizing utils into technical subfolders (utils/date/, utils/string/)
Response explains why technical-role grouping destroys locality
Response recommends moving utilities to their owning domain modules instead
Response identifies that utils/ is a complexity graveyard and the real fix is domain-based organization
Response applies the deletion test or locality test to justify the recommendation

evals.expected_output: The skill should handle a minimal/ambiguous trigger gracefully — it should ask a clarifying question to determine whether the user wants Mode A (diagnose existing code) or Mode B (design new architecture), not just pick one arbitrarily or produce a generic overview
expectations: Response does not arbitrarily pick Mode A or Mode B without user context
Response asks at least one clarifying question to determine the user's situation
Response does not produce a generic architecture lecture without understanding the user's needs

evals.prompt: I have a FastAPI Python service. The routes call SQLAlchemy models directly. Services layer is thin — basically just forwarding calls. We added a utils/ module last year and it now has 40 functions. We've been talking about refactoring for months but can never agree on how. One engineer wants Clean Architecture, another wants vertical slices, another wants to 'just add more layers'. The codebase is about 8000 lines. Can you help us think through this?
evals.expected_output: Mode A: Runs scripts (or notes it would run them), identifies the shallow service layer, utils folder, and direct SQLAlchemy-in-routes as architecture problems. Presents candidates with proper format. Does NOT pick Clean Architecture, vertical slices, or more layers without doing the diagnosis first. Ends with 'Which of these candidates interests you most?'
expectations: Response routes to Mode A — there's an existing codebase with structural problems
Response identifies at least 2 of: shallow service layer, utils folder bloat, infrastructure bleed (SQLAlchemy in routes)
Response does NOT just say 'use Clean Architecture' or endorse one of the team's existing options without diagnosis
Response presents structured candidates for the user to choose from
Response does NOT propose a generic naming refactor (renaming layers) as an architectural fix
